video_title: OpenAI Codex lead on the new shape of product work | Andrew Ambrosino
gist: OpenAI's Codex product/engineering lead Andrew Ambrosino argues that because AI has made software implementation cheap, the scarce input has flipped to 'taste' and curation, collapsing PM/design/engineering roles into high-agency builders. He gives concrete OpenAI adoption data (nearly 100% of employees use Codex weekly, 5M+ weekly active users, 6x growth since January) and details how Codex is evolving from a developer tool into a general knowledge-work 'home base' that uses computer use, connectors, and app extensions.

insights.claim: Nearly 100% of OpenAI's entire company (not just engineers) uses Codex weekly, indicating internal product-market fit far beyond the developer audience.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Internal full-company adoption is a leading signal that AI coding agents are becoming general knowledge-work tools, expanding Codex's addressable market beyond engineers.
insights.quote: 90% of people at Opening Eye use Codex. Not 90% of engineers. That's 90% of the entire company.

insights.claim: Codex has over 5 million weekly active users and usage has grown 6x since January, with the host suggesting the figure is rapidly becoming outdated.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Steep usage growth quantifies how fast agentic coding tools are scaling, relevant for sizing the AI dev-tools market and competitors like Cursor and Claude Code.
insights.quote: Since this January, Codeex usage has grown 6x. They currently have over 5 million weekly active users.

insights.claim: Ambrosino stated an explicit goal to make Codex the best desktop app that has ever existed, setting an unusually high quality bar so opening it becomes a reflexive default like opening a browser tab.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Signals OpenAI's ambition to own the desktop knowledge-work surface, competing with browsers and IDEs as the default daily-driver app.
insights.quote: The quality bar for Codex had to be so high that there was never like a hesitation that you have opening this app to do the next thing

themes.theme: Inversion of the product process: taste over implementation

insights.claim: Because AI has made implementation cheap, the expensive, scarce input in product development has inverted from coding to 'taste' and the curation of many parallel attempts.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Reframes where labor value concentrates: investors and operators should weight teams' curation/judgment capacity over raw engineering headcount.
insights.quote: The implementation is actually not the expensive part anymore. It's dare I say taste.

insights.claim: The old product process derisked expensive implementation upfront via docs, research, and prototypes, but that logic collapses once anyone can stand up any feature cheaply by talking to a model.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Workflows and tooling built around upfront derisking (heavy PRD/spec phases) face obsolescence; bet on tools that support rapid parallel building plus curation.
insights.quote: you want to derisk all implementation up front through documents through research through prototypes because prototypes and designs are cheaper was kind of the the assumption there uh and that's changed

insights.claim: Inside OpenAI, a single needed feature may have ~90 uncoordinated teams independently prototyping it, shifting the leadership job to curating and folding the best of those attempts together.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Illustrates a new 'multiplayer exploration' org model where coordination/curation, not generation, is the bottleneck — relevant to how AI-native orgs structure work.
insights.quote: I'm sure there are 90 different uncoordinated teams like implementing and trying

insights.claim: Ambrosino rejects the popular 'PRDs are dead, prototypes are in' claim, arguing that when implementation is abundant the key skill is choosing the right medium (document for product clarity, prototype for stress-testing interactions).
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Counters a widespread tech-Twitter consensus; teams that abandon written specs entirely may over-anchor on polished prototypes and ship the wrong thing.
insights.quote: you've seen many product leaders say PRDS are dead prototypes are in and I actually don't believe this at all

insights.claim: Polished, production-looking prototypes now decouple visual fidelity from process maturity, so a slick prototype can wrongly signal 'ready to ship' when assumptions are still unvalidated.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Teams need explicit process-stage labeling to avoid premature launches; a risk to watch as cheap prototyping floods orgs with deceptively finished-looking artifacts.
insights.quote: oh, visually it's ready for prod, but it's not actually the right model of of where the research is going or what users are asking for or what's right for the business

insights.claim: Ambrosino defines taste as more than aesthetics — including systems thinking, fit within a larger theme, how to present information, and choosing what to build when anything can be built.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Operationalizes the vague 'taste' buzzword into evaluable components, useful for hiring and assessing AI-native product talent.
insights.quote: what should this be like if we can build anything like what's what's the what's the goal here and how do we how do we get there that I think is like actually the real taste question here

themes.theme: Why AI is bad at design (and whether it will improve)

insights.claim: AI lags at design because design is harder to grade than code — there's no clean reward loop like 'does it compile,' since human taste is part of the feedback mechanism.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Suggests design quality remains a durable human moat and a slow area for model improvement; design-heavy products are less immediately disruptable by AI.
insights.quote: design's a little bit harder to grade um than than software... because the human aspect of taste is is like part of the feedback mechanism you need

insights.claim: Labs historically prioritized making models good at coding because correct code accelerates AI research itself, whereas design improvements aren't directly in that research flywheel.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Explains the structural reason coding capability outpaces design; investors can expect coding-adjacent AI value to compound faster than design automation.
insights.quote: the labs historically invest in making their models good at things that accelerates AI research

insights.claim: Good design requires novelty and cultural awareness, unlike software where models should over-index on known patterns — a model that perfectly reproduces Linear's website is not actually solving the design challenge.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Frames novelty as a harder-to-automate property of design, supporting a thesis that creative/original product work retains human value longer.
insights.quote: if I have a model that outputs linear's website every time that's not the challenge here

insights.claim: The hardest unsolved design problem for AI is the abstraction layer linking visual design to code semantics — e.g., understanding that two visually different components share an interaction pattern so a rebrand updates meaning, not just 263 components one by one.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Pinpoints a specific capability gap where current models fall short, useful for assessing claims of 'AI does design' and where the next frontier of value sits.
insights.quote: The deep version is like the semantics between these two things that look different... I think like that is still feeling a little bit out out of reach with the current technology

themes.theme: Role collapse and the future of PM/design/engineering

insights.claim: Ambrosino proposes that a person's role is now defined by the average of what they spend time on rather than fixed titles — if most work is PM work, you're a PM 'for now.'
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: A new mental model for org design and hiring in AI-native companies, where role fluidity replaces rigid functional silos.
insights.quote: if you average up all of the things that somebody on our design team does... on average, like their dots over here

insights.claim: The Codex org has seen more role collapse than other parts of OpenAI because it was a technical product where designers 'speak engineer' and PMs write code; Ambrosino notes designer Alexander has a CS master's degree.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Suggests role collapse advances fastest in technical-product teams, a pattern that may spread as more teams adopt AI tooling.
insights.quote: our designers speak engineer right our product managers speak technical language and write code

insights.claim: Ambrosino warns it is a 'terrible idea' to eliminate the product role and turn everyone into a 'builder,' because doing so abandons hard-won product discipline, best practices, and accumulated process knowledge.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Pushes back on the 'death of the PM' narrative; companies cutting product functions wholesale may destroy institutional capability — a risk for orgs over-rotating on the builder meme.
insights.quote: I've heard a lot of companies be like we're getting rid of the product role, which I think is, by the way, a terrible idea.

insights.claim: Ambrosino argues every discipline still has a real skill component and managers will not go away, because not everyone can work on everything in both breadth and depth — 'you can use Excel but you cannot work on the finance team.'
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Tempers the most extreme role-collapse predictions; specialization and management persist even as boundaries blur.
insights.quote: yes, you can use Excel you but you cannot work on the finance team, right?

insights.claim: What's eroding is gatekeeping tied to specific tools (assembly language, TypeScript syntax, Figma auto-layout), not the underlying roles — effectiveness is decoupling from mastery of exact tools.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Implies talent value is shifting from tool proficiency to mindset and judgment, reshaping how to evaluate and develop people.
insights.quote: there have always been parts of these roles that... are like, well, no, this like being good at this role is being good at this tool. And I think that's what's kind of starting to erode.

insights.claim: Both ICs and managers are now effectively managers — an IC no longer types code character by character but manages agents and work streams, while managers do the same at a different granularity.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Redefines the IC role around agent orchestration, relevant to how productivity and headcount are measured in AI-native teams.
insights.quote: If you're an IC, you're not typing code out character by character... you are managing agents

themes.theme: Team structure, hiring, and zone defense for product

insights.claim: The Codex desktop team is double-digit engineers, roughly half that many designers, and a few product people, staffed heavily with ex-founders and high-taste 'founder-shaped' people; OpenAI lets teams grow large and mostly ICs rather than adding management.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Concrete benchmark for how a frontier AI-native team is composed — small, IC-heavy, founder-profile talent — useful for org design comparisons.
insights.quote: we got a team uh double digits of engineers probably half that on the design side. um you know few product people

insights.claim: Ambrosino uses a 'zone defense' model for product: two product people working too closely is a bad signal; they should spread out to maximize company coverage and fill gaps, since top-down year-long planning no longer works amid chaos.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: A practical operating model for product orgs in fast-moving AI environments where curation and steering replace centralized planning.
insights.quote: if two product people are working too closely that's often not a good signal

insights.claim: The most valuable person now is the high-agency, high-taste individual who can take an idea from inception to done and discern signal from noise given unlimited tokens — this is the core hiring profile.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Defines the talent archetype winning in AI-native companies; a hiring and capital-allocation signal for who delivers outsized value.
insights.quote: you're going to have unlimited tokens and I don't like we can't just be doing slop like you need to be able to determine what's signal, what's noise

themes.theme: Planning, model timing, and building ahead of capability

insights.claim: OpenAI keeps long-range plans deliberately hazy because adding precision to a 9-month plan creates 'false precision' that wastes time — anything planned in November may have been true for December but not for what actually happened.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Roadmap precision is a liability in fast model-progress environments; the shorter-term-equals-more-detail heuristic guides planning under uncertainty.
insights.quote: any amount of precision that you add to a 9-month plan right now is false precision

insights.claim: Whether a feature is good now depends mostly on whether the model is smart enough yet, not on the feature's shape — so the playbook is to prototype many features, ship the ready ones, and re-test the rest each time models leap.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: A reusable build strategy: maintain a backlog of pre-built features that activate on model upgrades, decoupling product success from product design.
insights.quote: the whole premise of whether features were good or not were based on whether they were smart enough, not the shape of them

insights.claim: Ambrosino is highly confident the February Codex app would have failed in the market if released in November — only the model improvements between November and February changed the outcome, with the product shape unchanged.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Timing relative to model capability can be the dominant variable in product success — a key risk/timing lesson for AI product launches.
insights.quote: the Codex app that we released in February, if that had been ready in November, it would have absolutely failed in the market

insights.claim: Ambrosino draws a continuity line from Operator to Atlas to Codex to ChatGPT as fundamentally the same agentic feature, where re-releasing with stronger intelligence changes the outcome despite identical shape; the original Codex web was 'too AGI-pilled' for its moment while local Claude Code worked better.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Failed-then-revived features signal a pattern: don't kill ambitious features as 'bad,' they may just be early — relevant to evaluating AI product roadmaps and competitor moves.
insights.quote: we were too agi pilled for the moment

themes.theme: Autonomous development frontier and model limitations

insights.claim: 100% of OpenAI's product is now AI-written code by last year's goalposts, so the meaningful question has shifted to whether code is written supervised versus unsupervised.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Reframes the 'how much code is AI-written' metric; the real frontier is autonomy/supervision level, a better gauge of AI engineering maturity.
insights.quote: 100% of our product right now is AI written code. So the question is more like, well, okay, fine. Is is the code written supervised versus unsupervised

insights.claim: A key blocker to fully autonomous development is that all current models increase code complexity and are bad at deleting code; Ambrosino publicly asks research teams to make models better at deleting code.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Identifies a concrete model weakness (code deletion / complexity reduction) gating autonomous software development — a capability gap to track across labs.
insights.quote: If research is listening at any company, please make the models better at deleting code.

insights.claim: OpenAI is not yet at fully autonomous self-improving software (a loop that listens to Twitter/Slack/email and improves the app on its own) but is actively trying to get there.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Sets a realistic boundary on current autonomous-agent capability versus hype, with stated intent to close the gap.
insights.quote: I don't think we're at a place yet where we're like, we're just got to set up a loop that's like improve the app... we're not there yet, but we are we are we're trying to make it happen

themes.theme: Codex as general knowledge-work platform: computer use, connectors, extensions

insights.claim: When OpenAI tried building separate Codex-powered surfaces for non-engineering personas (in ChatGPT desktop, Atlas browser), nobody would leave the Codex app — proving the developer-tool versus general-knowledge-tool split is more nuanced than either/or.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Supports consolidating Codex and ChatGPT into one 'home base' rather than persona-specific apps — a directional signal for OpenAI's product strategy.
insights.quote: the most annoying problem happened which is nobody would leave the Codex app for the apps that were allegedly for these other personas

insights.claim: OpenAI's strategy is to make Codex/ChatGPT a 'home base' that uses other specialty tools rather than rebuilding them — e.g., the app talks directly to a Microsoft Excel add-in for financial modeling rather than relying on its built-in spreadsheet editor.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Positions OpenAI as an orchestration layer over existing software rather than a replacement, with implications for incumbents like Microsoft and vertical SaaS.
insights.quote: this thing should be a home for you where you start work, you end work, you automate work, and it uses whatever you need to do

insights.claim: Codex autonomously edited launch videos for OpenAI by recognizing the editor used Premiere Pro, editing the backing files, and then building itself a Premiere Pro extension to send commands to the app — despite Codex having no video-editing UI.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Demonstrates emergent agentic capability to bridge into arbitrary specialty software via self-built extensions — a powerful, under-appreciated expansion vector.
insights.quote: what Codex then did was built itself an extension that could be installed into Premiere Pro that it could then talk to

insights.claim: Codex's computer-use can take over a user's machine and click through tedious interfaces (like Google Cloud Console pub/sub setup) even without a dedicated connector, as the host experienced building an email-spam-filtering app.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Computer use lets agents bypass missing API integrations entirely, broadening automation reach and reducing dependence on pre-built connectors.
insights.quote: It's like I don't care if you don't have a connector, man. I'll just start clicking.

insights.claim: Codex can act like an autonomous personal assistant ('open claw'-style) — users set up scheduled tasks via natural language (e.g., a daily Slack brief across 3,000 channels) and coach it conversationally without editing config.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Shows the agentic-assistant use case maturing inside Codex, expanding it from coding into recurring personal-workflow automation.
insights.quote: I will see the daily brief that I have from like everything from the 3,000 Slack channels that I'm in

insights.claim: OpenAI productizes emergent user behavior: when many users independently build similar personal systems (e.g., memory via Obsidian or Notion mind palaces), the team folds the common pattern into a first-class feature like native memory.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: A roadmap-generation mechanism — watch which DIY user workflows OpenAI absorbs to predict future native features and which third-party tools get displaced.
insights.quote: there should be a memory feature that does that for you right that's pretty generic

insights.claim: OpenAI is pursuing two inverse integration models at once: letting Codex/ChatGPT use external web apps via connectors/computer use/extensions, and (per Dan Shipper's pitch) letting users run their SaaS apps inside Codex so the agent can add functionality.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Frames two competing/complementary agentic-app architectures; the winner shapes how SaaS apps surface inside AI agents and where value accrues.
insights.quote: those are kind of like two models that are almost inverse of each other that we're doing a lot with both at the same time

insights.claim: OpenAI's in-app browser evolved from a janky Electron-based developer-only front-end testing tool to a multi-tab browser on their OWL stack (which also powers the Atlas browser) with enterprise security for logging into real websites.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Reveals the shared browser infrastructure (OWL) across OpenAI's agentic products and the technical path toward agent-controlled browsing at enterprise grade.
insights.quote: we we switched over to our owl owl stack which had powered the Atlas browser and so now you know multitab and we've got enterprise security

themes.theme: Adoption psychology and durable career advice

insights.claim: Ambrosino is wary of predicting how things will be because the high-agency, self-figuring-out behavior that works at OpenAI is self-selected and won't generalize to most of the population, who are not early adopters.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: A reality check on AI-tool adoption curves: frontier-company usage overstates mainstream readiness, relevant to TAM and diffusion timing.
insights.quote: the people who are here are self- selecting for like... I'm somebody who just figures out the next thing to do and that's just not like most of the population will not be an early adopter of things

insights.claim: Ambrosino's core career advice for the AI era: do not get married to your exact process or tool mastery; get married to the outcomes you are uniquely able to deliver and change your process freely to try things.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: A durable principle for talent resilience as AI commoditizes tool skills — value migrates to outcome ownership over process attachment.
insights.quote: do not get married to your exact process. Get married to like the outcomes that you are uniquely able to deliver

insights.claim: Ambrosino describes 10-15 years of repeated failure (selling a startup 'for parts' in heavily regulated spaces, struggling AI tooling at another locked-down-industry startup) before Codex worked, attributing success to skill, passion, and market timing aligning.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Reinforces that breakout product success often follows long failure plus timing, tempering survivorship-bias narratives about AI winners.
insights.quote: I failed for like I don't know somewhere between 10 and 15 years before getting to this point

insights.claim: Ambrosino emphasizes OpenAI's brutally honest internal feedback culture (proposed product shapes get 2,000-message Slack threads about how stupid they are) as the reason the external product turns out well.
insights.speaker: guest, Andrew Ambrosino (Codex product/eng lead)
insights.so_what: Cultural signal: high-candor internal iteration may be a competitive advantage in AI product development worth assessing in companies.
insights.quote: throw that in slack and there's like a 2,000 message thread about how stupid we are
